perturbation.original_text: 4. Return of Information. Upon request of the disclosing party, --------------------- the receiving party agrees to promptly return all documents furnishes to it by the disclosing party, together with all copies thereof in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Return of Information. Upon request of the disclosing party, --------------------- the receiving party agrees to return all documents furnished to it by the disclosing party, together with all copies thereof in its possession, within 90 days of the request.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return of information to a fixed 90-day period introduces potential inconsistencies. California law requires the return of trade secrets promptly, and a 90-day period could be deemed unreasonable depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires trade secrets to be protected and returned promptly upon request. A 90-day delay may violate the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection and allow misuse of the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 90-day window for returning documents, which contradicts the requirement for prompt return of trade secrets under California law. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'California law requires the return of trade secrets promptly,' and a 90-day delay could be deemed unreasonable. The Law Explanation reinforces this by noting that the delay 'may violate the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.2 (a), allows for injunctions against misappropriation, implying a need for swift action, which the 90-day delay undermines.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement's term to one year could be problematic if the confidential information remains a trade secret beyond that period. California's trade secret law offers protection as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the NDA to one year might not adequately protect information that continues to meet the definition of a trade secret beyond that time, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures required for maintaining trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the term of the agreement from five years to one year. According to the Law Explanation, this change may conflict with maintaining trade secret protection if the confidential information remains a trade secret beyond the one-year period. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, states that the Act protects trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation further elaborates that California's trade secret law offers protection as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret. Therefore, limiting the NDA to one year might not adequately protect information that continues to meet the definition of a trade secret beyond that time, thus contradicting the need to take reasonable measures to maintain trade secret protection under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to immediately notify the other party in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information of the other party which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to notify the other party of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information of the other party which may come to its attention, within 60 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period to 60 days for misuse of confidential information is problematic. California law mandates immediate action to protect trade secrets. A 60-day delay could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires prompt action to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Delaying notification by 60 days may impede the disclosing party's ability to seek timely injunctive relief, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the law regarding trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 60-day delay for notifying the other party of misuse or misappropriation of confidential information, contradicting the requirement for immediate action implied by trade secret law. The original text requires immediate notification. The change undermines the prompt action needed to protect trade secrets, potentially impeding the disclosing party's ability to seek timely injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' but does not explicitly state 'immediate action'. However, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that delaying notification by 60 days is problematic and may violate the intent of trade secret protection laws, thereby creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.explanation: Having the contract signed by a "Senior Janitor" on behalf of Pacific Bell Information Services (PBIS) raises concerns about proper authorization and capacity to enter into the agreement. This can violate general contract law principles of agency and authority.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law states that signatures from the chairman of the board, president, or any vice president and the secretary, any assistant secretary, the chief financial officer, or any assistant treasurer are binding on a corporation. A janitor is unlikely to have such authority, therefore making the contract potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 313 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 1. CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 3. Directors and Management [300 - 318] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 313. Subject to the provisions of subdivision (a) of Section 208, any note, mortgage, evidence of indebtedness, contract, share certificate, initial transaction statement or written statement, conveyance, or other instrument in writing, and any assignment or endorsement thereof, executed or entered into between any corporation and any other person, when signed by the chairperson of the board, the president or any vice president and the secretary, any assistant secretary, the chief financial officer or any assistant treasurer of such corporation, is not invalidated as to the corporation by any lack of authority of the signing officers in the absence of actual knowledge on the part of the other person that the signing officers had no authority to execute the same. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 98, Sec. 6. (SB 351) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction because it replaces authorized signatories (as defined in CORP 313) with an unauthorized signatory ('Senior Janitor'). CORP 313 states that contracts signed by specific officers (chairperson of the board, president, vice president, secretary, assistant secretary, chief financial officer, or assistant treasurer) are binding. The 'Senior Janitor' does not fall under these titles, which creates a clear contradiction making the contract potentially unenforceable, as the law indicates who *can* sign, implying who cannot.
